food_subgroup: Cooked mixed vegetable dishes with spices

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mili Juli Sabzi is approximately 200 g, typically served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetables, Cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for cooked mixed vegetables with spices (sabzi preparation). Composite estimate based on typical sabzi ingredients: mixed seasonal vegetables (cauliflower, peas, carrots, beans), tomatoes, onions, green chillies, and cooking oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish). Spicy variant does not materially alter macronutrient profile. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect added salt in restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Cooked Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate from cooked vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mili Juli Sabzi. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous cooked mixed vegetable dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables consistently fall in the GI range 35–55 due to high fibre content, low starch, and presence of organic acids from tomatoes. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 7.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving / 100) / 100 = 7.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g, a 200 g restaurant serving contributes ~560 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and monitor total daily sodium intake against the recommended limit of 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mili Juli Sabzi has a low GI (~45) and provides approximately 2.6 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting glycaemic control; it is a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high vegetable fibre content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: With only 0.4 g saturated fat and 0 mg cholesterol per 100 g, and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from vegetable oil, this dish supports a heart-healthy diet; the high potassium (~310 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre further contribute to cardiovascular benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mili Juli Sabzi (Spicy Mixed Vegetables) is a classic North Indian vegetable dish made from a medley of seasonal fresh vegetables — typically including cauliflower, green peas, carrots, French beans, and potatoes — cooked with freshly ground whole spices, tomatoes, onions, and green chillies. The name 'Mili Juli' means 'mixed together' in Hindi, and 'Sabzi' means 'vegetables' or 'vegetable dish'. The spicy variant uses a more generous quantity of green chillies, red chilli powder, and aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish is typically stir-fried or sautéed in a small amount of oil, giving it a semi-dry texture with a vibrant, spiced coating on the vegetables. It is served as a side dish alongside roti, chapati, naan, or steamed rice in Indian households and restaurants. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, high-fibre, low-GI dish rich in vitamins A and C, potassium, and folate. The low saturated fat and zero cholesterol content make it suitable for cardiovascular health. Its low glycaemic index (~45) and high fibre content make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate in home preparation but may be elevated in restaurant servings. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Indian restaurants and is popular among the South Asian diaspora community.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked mixed vegetable dishes with spices; no exact entry for 'Mili Juli Sabzi (Spicy)' exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. The composite estimate is defensible and consistent with the described ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mixed non-starchy vegetables.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for cooked mixed vegetable sabzi preparations, adjusted for oil content (~5 g/100 g dish) and restaurant-level sodium. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero for vegan vegetable preparations. Vitamin D is 0.0 as plant-based cooked vegetable dishes contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN for vegetable dishes. Trans fat is 0.0 as the dish uses standard vegetable oil with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by formulation. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. GI set to 45 as an analogous estimate for cooked mixed non-starchy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. GL per serving = (45 × 7.8 × 200) / 10000 = 7.0. Serving size of 200 g reflects a typical restaurant side-dish portion. The spicy variant does not materially alter the macronutrient profile relative to standard mixed vegetable sabzi.
